items.q: How did westward expansion affect the Plains Indians?
items.correct: It destroyed their way of life as settlers, railroads and buffalo slaughter took their land, ending in confinement to reservations.
items.misconception: The Plains Indians easily co-existed with settlers and kept their lands.
items.random_wrong: The Plains Indians lived in eastern cities.
items.why: Students underestimate the conflict; expansion devastated Native societies through warfare, broken treaties and destruction of the buffalo.

items.q: Was the Battle of Little Bighorn a turning point in favour of Native Americans?
items.correct: It was a striking Native victory, but it provoked a fierce US response that soon crushed Plains resistance.
items.misconception: Little Bighorn secured lasting independence for the Sioux.
items.random_wrong: Little Bighorn was a battle of the American Civil War.
items.why: Learners assume the victory changed the outcome; instead it hardened US determination and accelerated the subjugation of the Plains nations.

items.q: Why did so many migrants head west after 1840?
items.correct: They were drawn by cheap land, gold, religious freedom and the idea of Manifest Destiny, despite the hardships of the journey.
items.misconception: Migrants moved west mainly to escape the Civil War.
items.random_wrong: Migrants went west because the government forced them.
items.why: Students misdate motives; much migration predated the Civil War and was driven by opportunity and ideology, not compulsion.

items.q: What belief justified American westward expansion?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Manifest Destiny. “The Monroe Doctrine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What event in 1849 drew thousands of migrants to California?
items.why: It's The Gold Rush. “The Civil War” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did the 1869 Transcontinental Railroad achieve?
items.correct: It linked the east and west coasts by rail
items.why: Correct answer: It linked the east and west coasts by rail. “It ended slavery” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What happened at the Battle of Little Bighorn in 1876?
items.correct: Sioux and Cheyenne defeated Custer's force
items.misconception: The US army wiped out the Sioux
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sioux and Cheyenne defeated Custer's force. “The US army wiped out the Sioux” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Transcontinental Railroad completed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1869 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
